rulings.Case Title: JOHN WILKINS VS. MARY BAYTOP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Vacate Void Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday March 27, 2014, line 1. DEFENDANT MARY BAYTOP'S Motion To Vacate Void Judgment. Denied. "If the complaint in the default action is sufficient to apprise the [defendant] of the nature of the [plaintiff's] demand, it is immaterial that it might have been subject to a demurrer for failure to make an allegation necessary to state a cause of action¿" (Molen v. Friedman (1998) 64 Cal.App.4th 1149, 1157.) Defendant's request for judicial notice is granted. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: PROVIDENCE CONDOMINIUM ASSOCIATION, A NON PROFIT VS. SPNS GOLDEN GATE, LLC, A DELAWARE LIMITED et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 27, 2014, line 4. CROSS DEFENDANT KENYON CONSTRUCTION, INC'S Motion For Determination Of Good Faith Settlement. Granted. G.P. Systems, Inc. has not met its initial burden of proof to show that the settlement was not made in good faith. (City of Grand Terrace v. Superior Court (1987) 192 Cal.App.3d 1251, 1261.) The settlement is within ballpark based on the Kenyon Construction, Inc.'s ("Kenyon") proportionate liability for the construction defects associated with its work on the project. The declaration of Bryce Gray is insufficient to substantiate that Kenyon should have allocated more to Cross-Complainants defense costs. Kenyon's objections are sustained. rulings.Case Title: RANDY LEE MCDOWELL VS. WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Moiton And Omtion Of Deft For Judgment On The Pleadings; Supporting Memo Of P&A
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 27, 2014, line 6. DEFENDANT WELLS FARGO BANK, N.A.'S Motion For Judgment On The Pleadings. Judge Goldsmith is recused. Per order of the Presiding Judge, this motion will be heard by Judge Stewart on the same date (March 27, 2014) at 1:30 pm in Dept. 613. The court will not provide a court reporter for this hearing. If any party wishes a reporter, the parties must meet and confer and agree on a single reporter. Judge Stewart has issued the following tentative ruling: Granted in part and denied in part. Granted without leave to amend as to the first cause of action. The First Amended Complaint ("FAC") does not plead facts establishing all the elements of malicious prosecution. (Johnson v. Ralphs Grocery Co. (2012) 204 Cal.App.4th 1097, 1103, "complaint [for malicious prosecution] must allege the institution or instigation of the proceeding, e.g., the accusation of a crime resulting in a preliminary hearing before a magistrate or an indictment on the charge.") The motion is granted without leave to amend as to the first cause of action in light of the fact that Plaintiff has not set forth facts in its opposition brief or any other pleadings establishing that further action beyond arrest occurred. Denied as to the second cause of action. The FAC adequately pleads facts establishing that Plaintiff was denied "equal accommodations and services based on his race." (FAC, pars. 18, 24, and 38.) Because the FAC states a cause of action for violation of the Unruh Act based on denial of services (opening a bank account and depositing a check), and not solely based on the fact that the police were called, the Court does not make a determination at this time as to whether the second cause of action is barred by the litigation privilege and/or Annunzio-Wiley Act. A more complete factual record needs to be developed. (Hagberg v. California Federal Bank FSB (2004) 32 Cal.4th 350, 376.) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order with the name and address of the prevailing party's counsel or the prevailing party if pro per in the top left of the first page of proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EHG)

rulings.Case Title: KIRSTEN COLEMAN VS. ELECTRO POP ROCKS, A PARTNERSHIP et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Ntc Of Mtn And Mtn To Quash Protective Order And To Compel Deft Michael Beeson'S Further Responses To Discovery And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Michael Beeson And His Attorney Spencer Martinez
rulings.Rulings: Matter on Calendar for Thursday, March 27, 2014, Line 2, PLAINTIFF KIRSTEN COLEMAN's motion To Quash Protective Order And To Compel Defendant Michael Beeson'S Further Responses To Discovery And Request For Monetary Sanctions Against Michael Beeson And His Attorney Spencer Martinez Pro Tem Judge Bruce Highman, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Order Granting Defendant Michael Beeson's Motion for Issuance of Protective Order that the Court filed on December 11, 2012, is not "quashed." But it is an order that merely allowed Mr. Beeson not to comply with the demand in the notice of his deposition to produce financial documents and not to answer financial questions at his deposition. It was based on the lack of evidence of partnership presented on the motion related to Mr. Beeson's deposition that the Court heard on December 11, 2012. It is not a general prohibition on financial discovery. It does not even prohibit requests for the same documents in a notice of another deposition or in a request for production of documents pursuant to CCP 2031 rather than in a deposition notice. In addition, it does not apply to trial. Whether to allow financial evidence at trial will be up to the trial judge. A hearing is required on plaintiff's motion to compel. The Court wants to know if all nonprivileged documents that support defendant Beeson's contention that EPR was at all times a sole proprietorship have been produced. As long as all such documents have been produced, the Court believes that the description of the documents in the interrogatory answer is satisfactory except that the Court is not sure if all documents withheld on grounds of attorney-client privilege, work product, or financial privacy are really privileged. The Court wants to know if all supporting documents withheld on grounds of attorney-client privilege or work product are documents solely written between Beeson and Ropers Majeski or solely written by Ropers Majeski and not shown to anyone else? The Court questions whether financial documents withheld on grounds of privacy are really privileged in this case where the Court has already found a triable issue of fact as to whether plaintiff was a partner. (See Rawnsley v. Superior Court (1986) 183 Cal.App.3d 86.) The Court wants to know if there are financial documents that support defendant Beeson's contention that EPR was at all times a sole proprietorship that have not been described in the interrogatory answer or that have not been produced in discovery. In making this inquiry, the Court emphasizes that it is not inquiring about all financial documents, only financial documents that support defendant Beeson's contention that EPR was a t all times a sole proprietorship. =302/jpt

rulings.Case Title: KENDRA L. TANACEA VS. RICHARD R. TAVERNETTI MD et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, March 27, 2014, line 15. DEFENDANT RICHARD TAVERNETTI'S MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. The motion is granted. Plaintiff's allegations that defendant unnecessarily implanted the hip prosthesis for financial gain because defendant had a financial interest in the manufacturer of the prosthesis is directly related to the manner in which professional services were provided. Accordingly, plaintiff must comply with CCP 425.13 to plead a claim for exemplary damages.
